title_en: Zakat and Fasting in Tabyin al-Haqaiq
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa dalam Tabyin al-Haqaiq
content_id:
Treatment az-Zayla'i tentang zakat dan puasa dalam Tabyin al-Haqaiq memeriksa pendapat-pendapat mazhab Hanafi atas dua rukun ini dengan analisis berlatar hadis yang menjadi ciri khas seluruh karyanya. Evaluasinya atas dalil hadis untuk setiap hukum Hanafi -- termasuk kesediaannya untuk mencatat ketika hadis yang dikutip ulama Hanafi lemah atau diperdebatkan -- menjadikan karya ini sangat berharga bagi para ulama yang terlibat dalam kajian hukum komparatif.

Bab zakat dibuka dengan perintah-perintah Al-Quran yang mendasar dan surat-surat Nabi tentang zakat yang tersimpan dalam koleksi hadis. Az-Zayla'i menjelaskan pendapat-pendapat Hanafi tentang setiap kategori harta yang wajib dizakati: emas dan perak (dan kekayaan moneter umumnya), barang dagangan, ternak, dan hasil pertanian. Definisi luas mazhab Hanafi tentang hasil pertanian yang wajib dizakati -- mencakup semua tanaman yang sengaja dibudidayakan -- dikontraskan dengan pembatasan Syafi'i pada biji-bijian dan kurma yang dapat disimpan.

Tentang apakah kekayaan anak kecil dikenai zakat, az-Zayla'i menyajikan pendapat Hanafi (zakat tidak wajib atas anak kecil) dan memeriksa dalil hadis yang dikutip oleh kedua pihak. Argumen Hanafi adalah bahwa zakat didefinisikan dalam hadis Mu'adz sebagai 'diambil dari yang kaya di antara mereka dan diberikan kepada yang fakir di antara mereka' -- dan seorang anak kecil tidak dapat 'kaya' dalam arti hukum karena ia tidak memiliki kapasitas hukum penuh. Az-Zayla'i mengevaluasi penalaran ini bersama hadis yang dikutip oleh mazhab lain untuk mewajibkan zakat atas kekayaan anak kecil, dan mempertahankan pendapat Hanafi sambil mengakui bobot nyata pandangan yang berlawanan.

Pendekatan Hanafi terhadap distribusi zakat dijelaskan secara rinci: mazhab Hanafi mengizinkan pemberian semua zakat kepada satu orang atau satu kategori penerima, berbeda dengan persyaratan mazhab Syafi'i untuk mendistribusikannya kepada delapan asnaf. Az-Zayla'i menjelaskan ini dengan mencatat bahwa ayat Al-Quran (at-Taubah 9:60) mendaftar delapan asnaf sebagai penerima yang berhak atas zakat -- mengindikasikan kelayakan, bukan kewajiban untuk mendistribusikan kepada semua. Prinsip memberikan di mana kebutuhan terbesar dapat dengan demikian diterapkan dalam satu asnaf atau bahkan kepada satu individu.

Untuk puasa, syarah az-Zayla'i atas Kanz ad-Daqa'iq mencakup pendapat-pendapat Hanafi beserta landasan dalilnya. Mazhab Hanafi mewajibkan niat malam untuk setiap hari Ramadan secara terpisah (konsisten dengan mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanbali) dan menentukan bahwa niat harus dibuat sebelum tengah hari puasa paling lambat -- pendapat yang lebih longgar daripada mewajibkannya sebelum Subuh. Batas akhir yang lebih longgar untuk niat ini didasarkan pada pandangan Hanafi bahwa tujuan niat adalah membedakan puasa dari makan secara pilihan, dan karena orang yang belum makan apapun dari Subuh hingga tengah hari belum melakukan sesuatu yang tidak sesuai dengan puasa, membuat niat sebelum tengah hari sudah cukup.

Tentang apa yang membatalkan puasa, mazhab Hanafi dianalisis oleh az-Zayla'i dengan perhatian khasnya pada dalil hadis. Tiga perbuatan pembatal puasa utama (makan, minum, hubungan seksual) ditetapkan oleh teks Al-Quran dan hadis. Pembatal puasa Hanafi tambahan meliputi: muntah yang disengaja dalam jumlah yang cukup; bekam ketika kekuatan seseorang melemah karenanya (pendapat Hanafi tentang bekam lebih bernuansa daripada larangan menyeluruh Hanbali -- bekam hanya membatalkan puasa ketika melemahkan seseorang, mengikuti hadis yang ditafsirkan mazhab Hanafi dalam arti terbatas ini).

Kaffarah karena sengaja membatalkan puasa Ramadan dijelaskan beserta landasan hadisnya: hadis terkenal dalam Bukhari dan Muslim yang menetapkan tiga tahap kaffarah (memerdekakan budak, puasa enam puluh hari berturut-turut, memberi makan enam puluh orang miskin). Az-Zayla'i mencatat pendapat distinktif Hanafi bahwa kaffarah dipicu oleh setiap perbuatan pembatal puasa yang disengaja -- bukan hanya hubungan seksual sebagaimana yang dipegang mazhab Syafi'i -- berdasarkan prinsip umum bahwa Al-Quran menetapkan puasa sebagai kewajiban mutlak yang pelanggarannya yang disengaja tanpa alasan layak mendapat penebusan paling serius.
content_en:
Az-Zayla'i's treatment of zakah and fasting in Tabyin al-Haqaiq examines the Hanafi school's positions on these pillars with the hadith-grounded analysis that characterizes his entire work. His evaluation of the hadith evidence for each Hanafi ruling — including his willingness to note when a hadith cited by Hanafi scholars is weak or disputed — makes the work particularly valuable for scholars engaged in comparative legal studies.

The zakah chapter opens with the foundational Quranic commands and the Prophet's letters on zakah preserved in the hadith collections. Az-Zayla'i explains the Hanafi positions on each category of zakatable property: gold and silver (and monetary wealth generally), trade goods, livestock, and agricultural produce. The Hanafi school's broad definition of zakatable agricultural produce — extending to all intentionally cultivated crops — is contrasted with the Shafi'i restriction to stored grains and dates.

On the question of whether the wealth of minors is subject to zakah, az-Zayla'i presents the Hanafi position (zakah is not obligatory on minors) and examines the hadith evidence cited by both sides. The Hanafi argument is that zakah is defined in the hadith of Mu'adh as being 'taken from the rich among them and given to the poor among them' — and a minor cannot be 'rich' in the legal sense because they lack full legal capacity. Az-Zayla'i evaluates this reasoning alongside the hadith cited by the other schools for obligating zakah on minors' wealth, and defends the Hanafi position while acknowledging the genuine weight of the opposing view.

The Hanafi approach to zakah distribution is explained in detail: the Hanafi school permits giving all zakah to one person or one category of recipients, unlike the Shafi'i school's requirement to distribute among all eight categories. Az-Zayla'i explains this by noting that the Quranic verse (at-Tawbah 9:60) lists the eight categories as recipients to whom zakah 'is for' — indicating eligibility, not a requirement to distribute among all of them. The principle of giving where the need is greatest can thus be applied within a single category or even to a single individual.

For fasting, az-Zayla'i's commentary on Kanz ad-Daqa'iq covers the Hanafi positions with their evidential basis. The Hanafi school requires a nighttime intention for each day of Ramadan separately (consistent with the Shafi'i and Hanbali schools) and specifies that the intention must be made before midday of the fast day at the latest — a more lenient position than requiring it before Fajr. This late deadline for intention is based on the Hanafi view that the intention's purpose is to distinguish the fasting from eating by choice, and since a person who has not eaten anything from Fajr to midday has not yet done anything incompatible with fasting, making the intention before midday is sufficient.

On what breaks the fast, the Hanafi school is analyzed by az-Zayla'i with his characteristic attention to the hadith evidence. The three primary fast-breaking acts (eating, drinking, sexual intercourse) are established by Quranic text and hadith. Additional Hanafi fast-breakers include: deliberate vomiting in sufficient quantity; cupping when the person's strength is weakened by it (the Hanafi position on cupping is more nuanced than the Hanbali blanket prohibition — cupping breaks the fast only when it weakens the person, following a hadith that the Hanafi school interprets in this limited sense).

The kaffarah for deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast is explained with its hadith basis: the famous hadith in Bukhari and Muslim establishing the three-stage kaffarah (freeing a slave, sixty-day consecutive fast, feeding sixty poor people). Az-Zayla'i notes the Hanafi distinctive position that the kaffarah is triggered by any deliberate fast-breaking act — not only sexual intercourse as the Shafi'i school holds — based on the general principle that the Quran establishes the fast as an absolute obligation whose deliberate violation without cause warrants the most serious expiation.
content_ar: Az-Zayla'i's treatment of الزكاة and الصيام in Tabyin al-Haqaiq examines the الحنفي school's positions on these pillars with the الحديث-grounded analysis that characterizes his entire work. His evaluation of the الحديث evidence for each الحنفي ruling — بما في ذلك his willingness to note عندما a الحديث cited by الحنفي العلماء is weak or disputed — makes the work particularly valuable for العلماء engaged in comparative legal studies. The الزكاة الباب opens with the foundational القرآني commands and النبي ﷺ's letters on الزكاة preserved in the الحديث collections. Az-Zayla'i explains the الحنفي positions on each category of zakatable property: gold and silver (and monetary الثروة generally), trade goods, livestock, and agricultural produce. The الحنفي school's broad definition of zakatable agricultural produce — extending to all intentionally cultivated crops — is contrasted with the الشافعي restriction to stored grains and dates. On the question of whether the الثروة of minors is subject to الزكاة, az-Zayla'i presents the الحنفي position (الزكاة is not obligatory on minors) and examines the الحديث evidence cited by both sides. The الحنفي argument is that الزكاة is defined in the الحديث of Mu'adh as being 'taken from the rich among them and given to the poor among them' — and a minor cannot be 'rich' in the legal sense لأن they lack full legal capacity. Az-Zayla'i evaluates this reasoning alongside the الحديث cited by the other schools for obligating الزكاة on minors' الثروة, and defends the الحنفي position بينما acknowledging the genuine weight of the opposing view. The الحنفي approach to الزكاة distribution is explained in detail: the الحنفي school permits giving all الزكاة to one person or one category of recipients, unlike the الشافعي school's requirement to distribute among all eight categories. Az-Zayla'i explains this by noting that the القرآني الآية (at-التوبة 9:60) lists the eight categories as recipients to whom الزكاة 'is for' — indicating eligibility, not a requirement to distribute among all of them. The principle of giving where the need is greatest can وهكذا be applied within a single category or even to a single individual. For الصيام, az-Zayla'i's commentary on Kanz ad-Daqa'iq covers the الحنفي positions with their evidential basis. The الحنفي school requires a nighttime intention for each day of Ramadan separately (consistent with the الشافعي and الحنبلي schools) and specifies that the intention must be made قبل midday of the fast day at the latest — a more lenient position than requiring it قبل Fajr. This late deadline for intention is based on the الحنفي view that the intention's purpose is to distinguish the الصيام from eating by choice, and بما أن a person who has not eaten anything from Fajr to midday has not yet done anything incompatible with الصيام, making the intention قبل midday is sufficient.
